nash_eq.name: Nash Equilibrium
nash_eq.summary: A profile of strategies where no player can profit by unilateral deviation.

def.text: A strategy profile $s^*=(s_1^*,\ldots,s_n^*)$ is a Nash equilibrium if $u_i(s_i^*,s_{-i}^*)\ge u_i(s_i,s_{-i}^*)$ for every player $i$ and every $s_i\in S_i$.
Equivalently, $s_i^*\in\arg\max_{s_i\in S_i} u_i(s_i,s_{-i}^*)$ for each $i$ — every player best-responds to everyone else.

int.text: No player regrets their choice given what the others did — it's a self-enforcing agreement.
It is a fixed point of the joint best-response map: nobody has a profitable unilateral deviation.

ex.text: In the Prisoner's Dilemma the unique pure Nash equilibrium is mutual defection $(D,D)$, even though $(C,C)$ Pareto-dominates it.
Matching Pennies has no pure equilibrium but a unique mixed one in which each player randomizes $(1/2,1/2)$.

form.text: Nash (1950) proved existence in every finite game by applying Kakutani's fixed-point theorem to the best-response correspondence on the compact convex set $\Delta(S)=\prod_i\Delta(S_i)$.
Equivalently, equilibria are the zeros of the Nash map where each player minimizes their regret $\max_{s_i'}u_i(s_i',s_{-i})-u_i(s)$.

app.text: Central to oligopoly theory, routing games, auction design, and evolutionary biology.
Provides the baseline benchmark for the Price of Anarchy and mechanism design guarantees.

prisoners_dilemma.name: Prisoner\'s Dilemma
prisoners_dilemma.summary: Canonical 2x2 game where individually rational defection leads to a Pareto-inferior outcome.

def.text: A symmetric $2\times 2$ game with payoffs satisfying $T>R>P>S$ and $2R>T+S$, where $C$ and $D$ denote Cooperate and Defect.
A standard payoff matrix is $\begin{pmatrix}(R,R)&(S,T)\\(T,S)&(P,P)\end{pmatrix}$ with e.g. $(T,R,P,S)=(5,3,1,0)$.

int.text: Each player strictly prefers to defect regardless of the opponent, yet mutual cooperation is strictly better than mutual defection.
It exposes the tension between individual incentives and collective welfare — rationality yields $(P,P)$ rather than $(R,R)$.

ex.text: With $(T,R,P,S)=(5,3,1,0)$, defecting yields $5$ vs cooperating $3$ if the other cooperates, and $1$ vs $0$ if the other defects, so $D$ strictly dominates $C$.
Real-world analogs: arms races, cartel cheating, doping in sports, and climate-change free-riding.

form.text: In the one-shot game $D$ is strictly dominant; the unique Nash equilibrium is $(D,D)$. In infinitely repeated play with discount factor $\delta\ge (T-R)/(T-P)$, cooperation can be sustained by trigger strategies (Folk Theorem).
Axelrod's tournaments showed that simple reciprocal strategies like Tit-for-Tat often prevail in iterated play.

app.text: Models public-goods provision, oligopoly collusion stability, and international treaty enforcement.
It motivates mechanism design, reputation, and long-run relationships.

dom_strat.name: Dominant Strategy
dom_strat.summary: A strategy that yields at least as high a payoff as any alternative regardless of others\' play.

def.text: $s_i$ strictly dominates $s_i'$ if $u_i(s_i,s_{-i})>u_i(s_i',s_{-i})$ for all $s_{-i}\in S_{-i}$; weak dominance replaces $>$ with $\ge$ (strict for some $s_{-i}$).
A strategy is (strictly) dominant if it (strictly) dominates every other strategy in $S_i$.

int.text: It's the best choice for player $i$ no matter what the opponents do — there is no scenario in which switching is better.
A dominant strategy makes strategic reasoning unnecessary: you should play it unconditionally.

ex.text: In the Prisoner's Dilemma, Defect strictly dominates Cooperate.
In a second-price (Vickrey) auction, truthfully bidding your value is a weakly dominant strategy.

form.text: If every player has a strictly dominant strategy, the resulting profile is the unique Nash equilibrium (a dominant-strategy equilibrium).
Iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies never removes a Nash equilibrium and commutes with order.

app.text: Dominant-strategy incentive compatibility (DSIC) is the gold standard in mechanism design — see VCG mechanisms.
Used to simplify analysis of auctions, voting rules, and contract design.

mixed_strat.name: Mixed Strategy
mixed_strat.summary: A probability distribution over a player\'s pure strategies.

def.text: A mixed strategy for player $i$ is $\sigma_i\in\Delta(S_i)$ with $\sigma_i(s_i)\ge 0$ and $\sum_{s_i}\sigma_i(s_i)=1$; expected payoff is $u_i(\sigma)=\sum_s\prod_j\sigma_j(s_j)\,u_i(s)$.
A mixed Nash equilibrium requires each player to be indifferent among all pure strategies in their support.

int.text: Randomization is optimal when predictability is exploitable — it keeps opponents guessing.
A mixing player must be indifferent among the pure strategies in the support, otherwise they'd collapse to a pure best response.

ex.text: In Matching Pennies, each player mixes $(1/2,1/2)$ giving expected payoff $0$.
Rock-Paper-Scissors has a unique symmetric mixed equilibrium $(1/3,1/3,1/3)$ with expected payoff $0$.

form.text: Nash's existence theorem guarantees a mixed equilibrium in every finite game. Harsanyi (1973) showed mixing is the limit of slightly perturbed pure-strategy play (purification).
Support characterization: if $\mathrm{supp}(\sigma_i^*)=T_i$ then $u_i(s_i,\sigma_{-i}^*)$ is constant on $T_i$ and $\ge$ its value off $T_i$.

app.text: Used in security games, poker bluffing, penalty-kick analysis, and randomized algorithms.
Essential for analyzing games lacking pure equilibria.

pure_strat.name: Pure Strategy
pure_strat.summary: A deterministic choice of an action from a player\'s strategy set.

def.text: A pure strategy for player $i$ is an element $s_i\in S_i$; equivalently a degenerate mixed strategy $\sigma_i$ with $\sigma_i(s_i)=1$.
In extensive-form games, a pure strategy is a complete contingent plan $s_i:H_i\to A$ assigning an action at every information set $h\in H_i$.

int.text: Pure strategies commit to one action without randomization.
They are the building blocks from which mixed strategies are constructed.

ex.text: In Battle of the Sexes, $(Opera,Opera)$ and $(Football,Football)$ are the two pure Nash equilibria.
Matching Pennies has no pure Nash equilibrium.

form.text: A finite game may have zero, one, or many pure Nash equilibria; existence is not guaranteed unlike the mixed case.
In supermodular games with monotone best responses, pure equilibria always exist (Topkis-Milgrom-Roberts).

app.text: Frequently used in coordination problems, supply-chain contracting, and engineering design.
Often preferred for interpretability when they exist.

zero_sum.name: Zero-Sum Game
zero_sum.summary: A game in which one player\'s gain exactly offsets the others\' losses.

def.text: A two-player game is zero-sum if $u_1(s)+u_2(s)=0$ for all $s\in S_1\times S_2$; it is constant-sum if $u_1(s)+u_2(s)=c$.
Equivalently it is described by a single payoff matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ with row player maximizing $x^\top A y$ and column player minimizing.

int.text: Pure conflict — whatever one side gains the other loses.
Optimal play is max-min / min-max: maximize your guaranteed payoff against a worst-case opponent.

ex.text: Chess, Go, and Matching Pennies are zero-sum (draws counted as split).
A rock-paper-scissors tournament with $\{-1,0,+1\}$ payoffs is zero-sum.

form.text: Von Neumann's Minimax Theorem: $\max_x\min_y x^\top A y = \min_y\max_x x^\top A y$ in mixed strategies, giving a unique value $v$.
The equilibrium can be computed by linear programming in polynomial time.

app.text: Underpins adversarial ML (GANs, robust optimization), pursuit-evasion, and security resource allocation.
LP duality between the two players' optimization problems is foundational.

coop_game.name: Cooperative Game
coop_game.summary: A game where binding agreements among coalitions are allowed; analysis focuses on joint payoffs.

def.text: A TU (transferable-utility) game is a pair $(N,v)$ where $N$ is the set of players and $v:2^N\to\mathbb{R}$ with $v(\emptyset)=0$; $v(S)$ is the worth of coalition $S$.
Solutions include the core, the Shapley value, the nucleolus, the bargaining set, and the kernel.

int.text: Players can form coalitions and freely redistribute the resulting surplus.
The question shifts from "what will I do" to "how should the joint value be divided".

ex.text: Bankruptcy/airport cost-sharing games have rich core and Shapley-value solutions.
A 3-player majority game with $v(S)=1$ iff $|S|\ge 2$ has empty core but a clear Shapley value $(1/3,1/3,1/3)$.

form.text: Key properties: superadditivity $v(S\cup T)\ge v(S)+v(T)$ for disjoint $S,T$; convexity $v(S\cup T)+v(S\cap T)\ge v(S)+v(T)$. Convex games always have a nonempty core containing the Shapley value.
Bondareva-Shapley theorem characterizes nonempty core via balanced collections of coalitions.

app.text: Cost allocation in utilities, revenue sharing in alliances, voting power, and collaborative ML.
Formalizes fairness notions like symmetry, efficiency, and additivity.

noncoop_game.name: Non-Cooperative Game
noncoop_game.summary: A game in which binding agreements are not possible; each player chooses independently.

def.text: Analysis is via strategic-form or extensive-form games $(N,\{S_i\},\{u_i\})$ with individual strategy spaces and utilities, solved by equilibrium concepts.
Solutions concepts include Nash, subgame-perfect, Bayesian, and correlated equilibria.

int.text: Every agent optimizes privately — cooperation must be self-enforcing.
It is the umbrella framework for most strategic modeling in economics and CS.

ex.text: Cournot duopoly, the Prisoner's Dilemma, and first-price auctions are non-cooperative.
Traffic routing is naturally non-cooperative — drivers minimize their own travel time.

form.text: Nash (1951) proved existence of mixed equilibria in finite non-cooperative games. Refinements tighten predictions in dynamic and incomplete-information settings.
Non-cooperative foundations for cooperative concepts are provided by the Nash program.

app.text: Default framework for IO, auction theory, algorithmic game theory, and multi-agent RL.
Self-enforcing cooperation is recovered via repetition or contracts.

minimax.name: Minimax Theorem
minimax.summary: In any finite two-player zero-sum game, max-min and min-max values coincide in mixed strategies.

def.text: Von Neumann (1928): $\max_{x\in\Delta^m}\min_{y\in\Delta^n} x^\top A y=\min_{y\in\Delta^n}\max_{x\in\Delta^m} x^\top A y=v(A).$
The common value $v$ is the value of the game; any saddle-point $(x^*,y^*)$ attains it.

int.text: Rational worst-case planning yields the same outcome for both sides.
It guarantees an optimal defensive strategy for each player in pure conflict.

ex.text: Matching Pennies has value $0$ with optimal mixes $(1/2,1/2)$ for both.
A $3\times 3$ RPS matrix has value $0$ with $(1/3,1/3,1/3)$ optimal.

form.text: Proof by LP duality: the row-player's LP $\max v\text{ s.t. }A^\top x\ge v\mathbf{1},\mathbf{1}^\top x=1$ is dual to the column-player's, so strong duality gives the minimax equality.
Generalizations include Sion's minimax theorem for quasi-concave/convex payoffs on compact convex sets.

app.text: Foundation of LP duality, robust optimization, statistical decision theory, and adversarial training of generative models.
Fictitious play converges to the minimax value in zero-sum games.

extensive_form.name: Extensive-Form Game
extensive_form.summary: A representation of sequential play using a rooted tree with information sets.

def.text: A tuple $\langle N,T,\prec,\iota,\mathcal H,\{u_i\}\rangle$: tree $T$ with precedence $\prec$, player function $\iota$, information partition $\mathcal H$ and payoffs on terminal nodes.
A pure strategy specifies an action at every information set; behavior strategies assign distributions over actions per info set.

int.text: Models who moves when and what each player knows at the time of their move.
Strategic and extensive forms are equivalent for strategic-form analysis but extensive form captures timing and information.

ex.text: The Centipede Game, Stackelberg leader-follower, and sequential bargaining are extensive form.
Any chess position is a node in the extensive-form game of chess.

form.text: Kuhn's theorem: in games of perfect recall, mixed and behavior strategies are outcome-equivalent.
Zermelo (1913) showed finite perfect-information games have a pure-strategy backward-induction solution.

app.text: Used to analyze negotiations, repeated interactions, and multi-stage mechanism design.
Backward induction and subgame perfection are natural here.

normal_form.name: Normal-Form Game
normal_form.summary: A simultaneous-move representation via strategy sets and payoff functions.

def.text: A tuple $\langle N,\{S_i\}_{i\in N},\{u_i:\prod_j S_j\to\mathbb{R}\}\rangle$; two-player finite games are often displayed as a payoff matrix.
Every extensive-form game induces a normal form over strategies (complete contingent plans).

int.text: All players choose once and simultaneously, without knowing others' choices.
It abstracts away timing and information flow to focus on strategy-level reasoning.

ex.text: The $2\times 2$ Prisoner's Dilemma matrix is the canonical normal form.
Any $n$-player finite game can be listed as an $n$-dimensional payoff tensor.

form.text: Nash equilibria in the normal form always exist in mixed strategies (Nash, 1950).
The normal form captures strategic equivalence classes coarser than extensive-form equivalence.

app.text: The normal form is the default representation for computing Nash equilibria algorithmically: the Lemke-Howson algorithm operates directly on bimatrix payoff tables. Most introductory game theory textbooks use normal-form analysis as their starting point.

In mechanism design, the designer first specifies the normal form — strategy spaces and payoff functions — before analyzing what equilibrium emerges. This abstraction underlies auction design, voting rule analysis, and contract theory.

bayesian_game.name: Bayesian Game
bayesian_game.summary: A game in which players have private information (types) drawn from a common prior.

def.text: A Bayesian game is $\langle N,\{\Theta_i\},p,\{A_i\},\{u_i(a,\theta)\}\rangle$ with type space $\Theta=\prod\Theta_i$ and prior $p\in\Delta(\Theta)$.
A strategy is $\sigma_i:\Theta_i\to\Delta(A_i)$; a Bayes-Nash equilibrium satisfies $\sigma_i^*(\theta_i)\in\arg\max_{a_i}\mathbb{E}_{\theta_{-i}|\theta_i}u_i(a_i,\sigma_{-i}^*(\theta_{-i}),\theta).$

int.text: Each player best-responds in expectation over the others' possible private information.
Private information is modeled as a "type" that parametrizes payoffs or beliefs.

ex.text: In a first-price auction with i.i.d. private values $v_i\sim U[0,1]$, the symmetric BNE bid is $b(v)=\frac{n-1}{n}v$.
Signaling models of labor markets (Spence) and insurance (Rothschild-Stiglitz) are Bayesian.

form.text: Harsanyi (1967-68) showed any game of incomplete information has a Bayesian equivalent via the type-space transformation.
Common-prior assumption links beliefs consistently through Bayesian updating.

app.text: Online advertising platforms run Bayesian auctions daily: each advertiser has a private value for a click, drawn from a distribution the platform estimates. The generalized second-price auction is analyzed as a Bayesian game of incomplete information.

In insurance markets, adverse selection creates a Bayesian game: buyers know their own risk type but insurers only know the population distribution. Rothschild-Stiglitz screening models analyze equilibrium contract menus under this information asymmetry.

repeated_game.name: Repeated Game
repeated_game.summary: A stage game played repeatedly, allowing histories to influence future play.

def.text: Given stage game $G$, the $T$-stage repeated game has payoff $\sum_{t=1}^T \delta^{t-1}u_i(a^t)$ with discount factor $\delta\in[0,1]$; $T=\infty$ gives the infinitely repeated game.
Strategies are history-dependent maps $\sigma_i:\bigcup_t H_t\to\Delta(A_i)$.

int.text: Players can condition today's play on past behavior, supporting reputations and threats.
Repetition transforms static dilemmas into opportunities for cooperation via credible punishments.

ex.text: Tit-for-Tat sustains cooperation in the infinitely repeated Prisoner's Dilemma for $\delta$ large enough.
Grim-trigger supports any individually rational feasible payoff in repeated oligopoly.

form.text: Folk Theorem: any individually rational feasible payoff can be sustained as a subgame-perfect equilibrium of the infinitely repeated game for $\delta$ close enough to $1$.
Finitely repeated games with unique stage-game equilibria collapse by backward induction to stage-game play.

app.text: Models long-run business relationships, cartel stability, and reputation in platforms.
Connects to reinforcement-learning convergence in multi-agent systems.

evo_gt.name: Evolutionary Game Theory
evo_gt.summary: Studies strategy frequencies in large populations under selection and mutation.

def.text: Strategies are heritable phenotypes; fitness equals expected game payoff against a random opponent from the current population state $x\in\Delta(S)$.
Solution concepts include evolutionarily stable strategies (ESS) and asymptotically stable rest points of evolutionary dynamics.

int.text: No foresight or rationality is required — selection substitutes for best-response reasoning.
Equilibrium emerges from differential reproduction of successful strategies.

ex.text: The Hawk-Dove game admits a mixed ESS at $x_H=v/c$ for value $v$ and cost $c$.
Sex-ratio evolution gives a $1:1$ ratio as an ESS (Fisher).

form.text: Every ESS is a Nash equilibrium of the symmetric game; the converse requires additional stability conditions.
Replicator dynamics link EGT to Lyapunov analysis and relative entropy.

app.text: Biology (animal conflicts, signaling), cultural evolution, learning in games, and multi-agent RL.
Explains seemingly irrational persistence of cooperation in animal populations.

ess.name: Evolutionarily Stable Strategy
ess.summary: A strategy that, if adopted by the population, cannot be invaded by any rare mutant.

def.text: $x^*$ is an ESS if for every $y\ne x^*$ there exists $\bar\varepsilon>0$ such that $u(x^*,\,\varepsilon y+(1-\varepsilon)x^*)>u(y,\,\varepsilon y+(1-\varepsilon)x^*)$ for all $\varepsilon\in(0,\bar\varepsilon)$.
Maynard Smith-Price equivalent: $u(x^*,x^*)\ge u(y,x^*)$ for all $y$, and if $u(x^*,x^*)=u(y,x^*)$ then $u(x^*,y)>u(y,y)$.

int.text: Once nearly everyone plays $x^*$, no small group of mutants can earn strictly more and thus they cannot spread.
ESS is a local stability refinement of symmetric Nash.

ex.text: Hawk-Dove: the mixed ESS is $x_H^*=v/c$ when $c>v$.
In Rock-Paper-Scissors, the unique symmetric Nash $(1/3,1/3,1/3)$ is not an ESS — populations cycle under replicator dynamics.

form.text: Every ESS is a Nash equilibrium; not every Nash equilibrium is an ESS. An ESS is asymptotically stable under replicator dynamics (Taylor-Jonker).
For matrix games, ESS $\Leftrightarrow$ $x^*$ is a local strict maximum of $u(x,x)$ on an invariant face.

app.text: Explains animal contests, plant seed strategies, honest signaling, and robust equilibrium selection.
Widely used to motivate equilibrium refinements in economics.

replicator.name: Replicator Dynamics
replicator.summary: ODEs describing how strategy frequencies evolve under selection proportional to fitness.

def.text: $\dot x_i = x_i\bigl(f_i(x)-\bar f(x)\bigr)$, where $f_i(x)=(Ax)_i$ is the fitness of pure strategy $i$ and $\bar f(x)=x^\top A x$ is the average.
The simplex $\Delta(S)$ and its faces are invariant under the flow.

int.text: Strategies doing better than average grow; those doing worse decline.
No rationality is assumed — the dynamics are a pure selection model.

ex.text: Rock-Paper-Scissors under replicator dynamics exhibits closed orbits around the center $(1/3,1/3,1/3)$.
In the Hawk-Dove game trajectories converge to the mixed ESS $x_H=v/c$.

form.text: For symmetric games, every ESS is asymptotically stable; strict Nash equilibria are asymptotically stable; interior Nash equilibria are Lyapunov-stable. Relative entropy to a Nash equilibrium is a Lyapunov function for doubly symmetric games.
Related to Lotka-Volterra equations via a nonlinear change of variables.

app.text: Used in population genetics, cultural learning, congestion adaptation, and multi-agent RL convergence analysis.
Models of social learning via imitation yield the same ODE.

stackelberg.name: Stackelberg Competition
stackelberg.summary: A sequential duopoly in which a leader commits to an output and the follower best-responds.

def.text: Leader chooses $q_1$ to maximize $\pi_1(q_1,q_2^*(q_1))$ where $q_2^*(q_1)\in\arg\max_{q_2}\pi_2(q_1,q_2)$ is the follower's best response.
The solution concept is subgame perfect equilibrium, obtained by backward induction.

int.text: Committing first can be valuable — the leader exploits the follower's rational response.
First-mover advantage arises when best responses slope downward.

ex.text: With linear demand $P=a-Q$ and zero cost, Stackelberg yields $q_1=a/2$, $q_2=a/4$, giving profits $a^2/8$ and $a^2/16$ — the leader earns more than Cournot's $a^2/9$.
Security games deploy randomized commitments against best-responding attackers (Stackelberg security).

form.text: Leader's program is a bilevel optimization $\max_{q_1}\pi_1(q_1, q_2^*(q_1))$ — generally nonconvex but tractable in standard demand systems.
Without commitment power, the game collapses to simultaneous Cournot.

app.text: Industrial organization, pricing, and entry deterrence.
Stackelberg security games deploy patrol schedules at airports (ARMOR, IRIS).

cournot.name: Cournot Competition
cournot.summary: A simultaneous-move duopoly/oligopoly with quantity as the strategic variable.

def.text: Firms simultaneously choose quantities $q_i\ge 0$ and receive profit $\pi_i=q_i P(Q)-c_i(q_i)$ where $Q=\sum_j q_j$.
The Cournot-Nash equilibrium solves $\partial\pi_i/\partial q_i=0$ for all $i$ simultaneously.

int.text: Each firm takes rivals' output as given and equates marginal revenue to marginal cost.
Equilibrium lies between monopoly and perfect competition.

ex.text: With linear demand $P=a-Q$ and zero cost, symmetric equilibrium is $q_i^*=a/(n+1)$ and price $P=a/(n+1)$; as $n\to\infty$, $P\to 0$.
Two firms with these parameters earn $\pi=a^2/9$ each.

form.text: Best response $q_i^*(q_{-i})=\tfrac{1}{2}(a-\sum_{j\ne i}q_j)$ in the linear case; intersection gives the Nash equilibrium.
As $n\to\infty$, the Cournot equilibrium converges to the competitive outcome (Walras).

app.text: The OPEC oil cartel's production decisions approximate Cournot competition among member nations. Each country chooses its output quantity knowing that aggregate supply affects the world oil price, and the model predicts how production quotas affect prices.

In the global semiconductor industry, major manufacturers like TSMC, Samsung, and Intel engage in quantity competition: each decides fab capacity independently, and the total installed capacity determines chip prices in downstream markets worldwide.

bertrand.name: Bertrand Competition
bertrand.summary: Simultaneous price-setting competition among firms producing a homogeneous good.

def.text: Firms simultaneously post prices $p_i$; demand goes entirely to the lowest price (split if tied). Profit is $\pi_i=(p_i-c)D(p_i)\cdot \mathbf 1\{p_i\le p_{-i}\}\cdot\text{share}.
The "Bertrand paradox" arises with identical goods and marginal cost $c$: the unique Nash equilibrium is $p_i=c$ for all $i$.

int.text: Price undercutting drives price to marginal cost with even as few as two firms.
Tiny asymmetries in cost or differentiation change the picture dramatically.

ex.text: Two symmetric firms with constant marginal cost $c$ both price at $c$ in equilibrium, earning zero profit.
Differentiated Bertrand with logit demand yields positive equilibrium markups.

form.text: With capacity constraints (Edgeworth-Bertrand) or product differentiation, equilibrium prices exceed marginal cost.
Kreps-Scheinkman: Cournot outcomes emerge as the subgame perfect equilibrium of a two-stage capacity-then-price game.

app.text: Explains why concentrated markets still exhibit competitive pricing if products are homogeneous.
Motivates product differentiation as a profit-protection strategy.

auction_theory.name: Auction Theory
auction_theory.summary: Study of resource allocation via bidding mechanisms.

def.text: Standard auction formats include first-price sealed-bid, second-price (Vickrey), English (ascending), and Dutch (descending).
With private values $v_i$ i.i.d.\ from $F$, a symmetric equilibrium bid in the first-price auction is $b(v)=\mathbb E[\max_{j\ne i}v_j\mid v_i=v]$.

int.text: Different formats trade off revenue, efficiency, and strategic complexity.
Under symmetric risk-neutral private values all four standard auctions yield the same expected revenue — the Revenue Equivalence Theorem.

ex.text: In a Vickrey auction, bidding $b_i=v_i$ is weakly dominant and the highest value wins paying the second-highest bid.
In eBay-style ascending auctions, proxy bidding implements the Vickrey outcome under IPV.

form.text: Revenue Equivalence (Myerson-Riley-Samuelson): any symmetric IPV auction allocating to the highest-valuation bidder with the losers paying zero has the same expected revenue.
Myerson's optimal auction uses virtual valuations $\psi(v)=v-\frac{1-F(v)}{f(v)}$ and allocates to the bidder with highest $\psi$ if $\ge 0$.

app.text: Used in spectrum auctions, online ad exchanges (GSP, VCG), treasury bonds, and art sales.
Basis for algorithmic mechanism design.

vickrey.name: Vickrey Auction
vickrey.summary: A sealed-bid auction where the highest bidder wins and pays the second-highest bid.

def.text: Winner: $i^*=\arg\max_j b_j$; payment: $p_{i^*}=\max_{j\ne i^*} b_j$; all others pay $0$.
With single-unit private values, truthful bidding $b_i=v_i$ is weakly dominant.

int.text: Because price is set by others, your bid only affects whether you win, not how much you pay — so truth-telling is optimal.
Incentive compatibility means bidders do not need to strategize over others' valuations.

ex.text: Values $(10,7,4)$: bidder 1 wins and pays $7$, netting surplus $3$.
Under symmetric IPV, expected revenue equals that of the first-price auction (Revenue Equivalence).

form.text: The Vickrey auction is a special case of the VCG mechanism for a single indivisible good.
Generalizes to combinatorial settings as VCG, charging each winner their marginal externality on others.

app.text: Historical use in stamp auctions; conceptual basis for GSP in sponsored-search ads and many computerized markets.
Cornerstone example in mechanism-design teaching.

mech_design.name: Mechanism Design
mech_design.summary: Designing the rules of a game so that equilibrium behavior yields desired outcomes.

def.text: A mechanism is $(M,g)$ with message space $M=\prod M_i$ and outcome function $g:M\to X$. It implements social choice function $f$ in equilibrium concept $\sigma$ if $g(\sigma^*(\theta))=f(\theta)$ for all type profiles $\theta$.
Goals typically include efficiency, budget balance, individual rationality, and incentive compatibility.

int.text: Design the rules backwards from the outcome you want.
Anticipate strategic play and shape incentives so truthfulness or efficiency emerges.

ex.text: The VCG mechanism implements efficient allocation in dominant strategies.
Optimal auctions (Myerson) maximize expected revenue subject to Bayesian incentive compatibility.

form.text: Revelation Principle: any outcome implementable by some mechanism is implementable by a direct, incentive-compatible mechanism $f:\Theta\to X$.
Myerson-Satterthwaite impossibility: no mechanism for bilateral trade is simultaneously efficient, individually rational, budget-balanced, and Bayesian incentive-compatible.

app.text: Spectrum auctions, matching markets (kidney exchange, school choice), online ad auctions, and procurement.
Central in algorithmic mechanism design and EC theory.

revelation.name: Revelation Principle
revelation.summary: Any equilibrium outcome of any mechanism can be reproduced by a direct truthful mechanism.

def.text: If mechanism $(M,g)$ implements $f$ in (Bayes-)Nash equilibrium $\sigma^*$, then the direct mechanism $f=g\circ\sigma^*$ is (Bayesian) incentive-compatible and implements $f$ truthfully.
Variants exist for dominant-strategy and Nash implementation.

int.text: Rather than searching over all possible message spaces, designers can restrict attention to direct truthful mechanisms without loss of generality.
It reduces mechanism design to choosing a social-choice rule subject to IC constraints.

ex.text: The Vickrey auction is the direct truthful version of the English ascending auction.
Any iterative bidding process implementing the same allocation has a direct-truthful counterpart.

form.text: Formally a "without loss of generality" theorem that massively reduces the design space.
Caveats: does not address computational tractability, multiplicity of equilibria, or full implementation.

app.text: Foundational for auction, voting, and contract design analysis.
Allows a clean separation between incentive compatibility and the social objective.

shapley.name: Shapley Value
shapley.summary: A unique fair allocation in cooperative games satisfying efficiency, symmetry, dummy, and additivity.

def.text: $\phi_i(v)=\sum_{S\subseteq N\setminus\{i\}}\dfrac{|S|!\,(|N|-|S|-1)!}{|N|!}\bigl(v(S\cup\{i\})-v(S)\bigr).$
Equivalently an average of player $i$'s marginal contribution over all orderings of $N$.

int.text: Each player is paid their expected marginal contribution if players join in a uniformly random order.
It is the unique payoff vector satisfying four natural fairness axioms.

ex.text: For the majority game $v(S)=\mathbf 1\{|S|\ge 2\}$ with $N=\{1,2,3\}$, $\phi=(1/3,1/3,1/3)$.
Airport cost-sharing: incremental runway costs yield Shapley values that equal the average cost-per-user.

form.text: Shapley (1953) characterized $\phi$ uniquely via efficiency ($\sum\phi_i=v(N)$), symmetry, dummy/null player, and linearity/additivity.
For convex games, the Shapley value lies in the core (Shapley, 1971).

app.text: Cost allocation, feature attribution in ML (SHAP values), power indices in voting, and collaborative learning.
Interpretable ML tool (SHAP) that has become standard practice.

core.name: Core (of a Cooperative Game)
core.summary: The set of coalition-stable payoff allocations.

def.text: $\text{Core}(v)=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^N:\sum_{i\in N}x_i=v(N),\;\sum_{i\in S}x_i\ge v(S)\;\forall S\subseteq N\}.$
No coalition can strictly improve by breaking off — every coalition earns at least what it can guarantee.

int.text: Allocations in the core are immune to any coalitional deviation.
Think of it as a set-valued solution generalizing individual rationality to every subgroup.

ex.text: In the majority game with three players, the core is empty.
In a market with one seller and many buyers, the core converges to the competitive equilibrium as the market grows (Debreu-Scarf).

form.text: Bondareva-Shapley Theorem: the core is nonempty iff the game is balanced — any balanced collection of weights satisfies $\sum_S\lambda_S v(S)\le v(N)$.
Convex games have nonempty cores with the Shapley value as the barycenter.

app.text: Market equilibria, assignment games, and fair resource allocation.
Provides a "no-blocking" stability concept across all coalitions.

coalition.name: Coalition Formation
coalition.summary: Study of which coalitions form and how they share surplus.

def.text: Partition function games $v:\mathcal P(N)\to\mathbb{R}$ assign value to each coalition within a coalition structure (partition).
Hedonic games assign each player a preference over coalitions containing them and study stable partitions.

int.text: Beyond "everyone cooperates", who teams up with whom and why?
Stability, fairness, and efficiency of partitions are all considered.

ex.text: In hedonic matching (roommate problem), no-blocking partitions may not exist.
In political coalitions, the Banzhaf and Shapley-Shubik power indices measure pivotal influence.

form.text: Core-stability, Nash-stability, and individual stability each capture different robustness concepts for partitions.
Even in simple hedonic games, computing stable partitions can be NP-hard.

app.text: Political alliances, R&D consortia, group formation in social networks, and team formation in distributed computing.
Connects to matching theory and hedonic preferences.

nash_bargaining.name: Nash Bargaining Solution
nash_bargaining.summary: The unique allocation satisfying Pareto-efficiency, symmetry, scale invariance, and IIA.

def.text: Given feasible set $F\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2$ and disagreement point $d$, $\phi(F,d)=\arg\max_{x\in F,\,x\ge d}(x_1-d_1)(x_2-d_2).$
Generalizes to $n$ players via the weighted Nash product $\prod_i(x_i-d_i)^{\alpha_i}$.

int.text: Maximize the product of gains from agreement relative to the outside option.
It is the unique "fair" solution under Nash's four axioms.

ex.text: Splitting \$1 with $d=(0,0)$: the Nash solution is $(0.5,0.5)$; if $d=(0.3,0)$, it becomes $(0.65,0.35)$.
Wage bargaining with threat points gives each side a share of the surplus proportional to bargaining strength.

form.text: Nash (1950) axiomatization: Pareto-efficiency + symmetry + invariance to affine utility transformations + IIA uniquely identifies the product-maximizer.
Rubinstein (1982) provided a non-cooperative microfoundation via alternating-offers bargaining.

app.text: Labor-management negotiations, trade agreements, and divorce settlements.
Workhorse in matching/search models of the labor market.

rubinstein.name: Rubinstein Bargaining
rubinstein.summary: A non-cooperative alternating-offers model of bargaining with a unique subgame-perfect outcome.

def.text: Two players alternate offers to split a pie of size $1$; discount factors $\delta_1,\delta_2\in(0,1)$ reflect impatience.
The unique SPE has player 1 offering $x_1^*=(1-\delta_2)/(1-\delta_1\delta_2)$ and it being accepted immediately.

int.text: Impatient players concede faster; first-mover advantage vanishes as frictions disappear.
The ability to delay is an implicit threat that drives the equilibrium split.

ex.text: With $\delta_1=\delta_2=\delta$, the proposer gets $1/(1+\delta)$ and the responder $\delta/(1+\delta)$.
As $\delta\to 1$, the outcome converges to the symmetric Nash bargaining solution $(1/2,1/2)$.

form.text: Proved by stationary backward-induction: equate each player's offer to what they could obtain by waiting one period and proposing themselves.
Provides the "Nash program" microfoundation for the Nash bargaining solution.

app.text: Microfoundations for search-matching, wage determination, and bilateral trade.
Extensions incorporate outside options, risk of breakdown, and incomplete information.

corr_eq.name: Correlated Equilibrium
corr_eq.summary: An equilibrium in which players obey recommendations from a common randomization device.

def.text: A distribution $\mu\in\Delta(S)$ is a correlated equilibrium if $\sum_{s_{-i}}\mu(s_i,s_{-i})\bigl(u_i(s_i,s_{-i})-u_i(s_i',s_{-i})\bigr)\ge 0$ for all $i,s_i,s_i'$.
Given the recommendation $s_i$, following it weakly dominates any deviation $s_i'$ in expectation over $s_{-i}\mid s_i$.

int.text: A trusted signal coordinates play — think of a traffic light rotating priorities.
Every Nash equilibrium is correlated, but correlated equilibria can achieve strictly higher welfare.

ex.text: In Chicken with payoffs $(0,0),(4,1),(1,4),(-5,-5)$, correlated play with $(D,S),(S,D),(S,S)$ each with probability $1/3$ gives expected payoff $10/3$ — higher than any Nash.
Traffic lights implement a correlated equilibrium between arriving drivers.

form.text: Aumann (1974): the set of correlated equilibria is convex, contains all Nash equilibria, and can be computed via linear programming.
No-regret dynamics converge to the set of correlated equilibria in every finite game (Hart-Mas-Colell).

app.text: Explains social norms and conventions as coordination devices.
Computationally attractive and learnable via simple algorithms.

spe.name: Subgame Perfect Equilibrium
spe.summary: A Nash equilibrium that also prescribes a Nash equilibrium in every subgame.

def.text: A strategy profile $\sigma^*$ is a subgame perfect equilibrium if its restriction to every subgame is a Nash equilibrium of that subgame.
Found via backward induction in finite games of perfect information.

int.text: Rules out non-credible threats by requiring rational play even off the equilibrium path.
A refinement of Nash that respects sequential rationality.

ex.text: In Stackelberg competition, the SPE is the leader-follower outcome obtained by backward induction.
In the entry-deterrence game, threats to fight that would be irrational ex post are not subgame perfect.

form.text: Selten (1965) introduced SPE; for finite perfect-information games it coincides with the backward-induction path.
Zermelo's theorem guarantees existence in finite perfect-information games.

app.text: Sequential bargaining, dynamic oligopoly, and repeated-game analysis.
Essential when credibility of continuation play matters.

pbe.name: Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium
pbe.summary: A refinement for dynamic games of incomplete information pairing strategies with beliefs.

def.text: A PBE is $(\sigma,\mu)$ such that (i) each $\sigma_i$ is sequentially rational given beliefs $\mu$ and (ii) $\mu$ is updated via Bayes' rule on the equilibrium path (and reasonably off-path).
Stronger refinements include sequential equilibrium (Kreps-Wilson) which requires beliefs to be the limit of fully mixed strategies.

int.text: Beliefs are an integral part of the solution — players best-respond given what they believe about private information at each decision point.
It is the natural refinement for signaling and reputation games.

ex.text: In Spence's signaling model, a PBE involves high-ability workers choosing education levels that low-ability workers would not imitate.
Separating and pooling equilibria in signaling games are classified by PBE.

form.text: Sequential equilibrium strengthens PBE by requiring consistent beliefs via a trembling approximation.
Intuitive criterion (Cho-Kreps) further refines signaling equilibria via off-path belief restrictions.

app.text: Job-market signaling, reputation models, bargaining under incomplete information, and cheap talk.
Used to analyze dynamic corporate finance and political-economy models.

trembling.name: Trembling-Hand Perfection
trembling.summary: An equilibrium robust to small probabilities of mistakes.

def.text: $\sigma^*$ is trembling-hand perfect if it is the limit of a sequence of totally mixed $\varepsilon$-equilibria as $\varepsilon\to 0$.
Equivalently, best-responding against small trembles ($\sigma_{-i}^\varepsilon$) still prescribes the original strategy.

int.text: Players may occasionally make mistakes, and equilibrium should remain optimal under this perturbation.
It rules out weakly dominated strategies that are fragile to tiny errors.

ex.text: Selten (1975) motivated the concept using a simple game where weakly dominated strategies survive Nash but not trembling-hand perfection.
In 2-player normal form games, trembling-hand perfection coincides with playing undominated best responses.

form.text: Every trembling-hand perfect equilibrium is a Nash equilibrium that uses no weakly dominated strategies.
In the extensive form, the analog is subgame-perfect trembling-hand perfection, strengthened further by sequential equilibrium.

app.text: Motivates robust mechanism design and evolutionary robustness.
Commonly invoked to discard weakly dominated equilibria.

battle_of_sexes.name: Battle of the Sexes
battle_of_sexes.summary: A coordination game with asymmetric preferences over the two coordinated outcomes.

def.text: Payoff matrix $\begin{pmatrix}(2,1)&(0,0)\\(0,0)&(1,2)\end{pmatrix}$ representing two activities each player prefers differently but both prefer coordinating.
Two pure Nash equilibria and one mixed Nash equilibrium.

int.text: Both want to be together; they disagree on where.
Highlights equilibrium selection challenges in coordination games.

ex.text: Pure equilibria: $(O,O)$ and $(F,F)$ where one player prefers one and the other prefers the other.
Mixed equilibrium has player 1 choose $O$ with probability $2/3$ and player 2 choose $O$ with probability $1/3$.

form.text: Payoff-dominance selects $(O,O)$ for player 1 and $(F,F)$ for player 2 — they disagree. Risk-dominance and communication can break the tie.
Correlated equilibrium expands the set of welfare-improving outcomes.

app.text: The VHS versus Betamax format war of the 1980s was a Battle of the Sexes: both Sony (Betamax) and JVC (VHS) preferred different standards, but consumers desperately needed a single coordinated outcome. VHS won despite Betamax's technical superiority.

The choice between iOS and Android for app developers is a Battle of the Sexes: developers prefer the platform with more users, and users prefer the platform with more apps. Network effects lock in coordination on whichever platform gains initial momentum.

hawk_dove.name: Hawk-Dove Game
hawk_dove.summary: A symmetric 2x2 conflict game used to model animal contests.

def.text: Payoffs: $u(H,H)=(v-c)/2$, $u(H,D)=v$, $u(D,H)=0$, $u(D,D)=v/2$ for resource value $v$ and fight cost $c$.
When $c>v$, the ESS is mixed with $x_H^*=v/c$.

int.text: Hawks always escalate; Doves always retreat. Neither pure strategy is evolutionarily stable when escalation is costly.
Populations settle at a mix where the expected payoff to Hawks and Doves equalizes.

ex.text: With $v=2,c=4$: $x_H^*=1/2$, giving expected payoff $1/2$ for both types.
Observed frequencies of aggressive vs. submissive individuals in many species approximate mixed ESS.

form.text: Equivalent to Chicken in economics when costs exceed rewards.
Under replicator dynamics, trajectories converge to the mixed ESS from any interior start.

app.text: Animal-conflict theory, business "chicken" dynamics, and territorial disputes.
Foundational example in evolutionary stability analysis.

stag_hunt.name: Stag Hunt
stag_hunt.summary: A coordination game where mutual cooperation on the risky action maximizes welfare.

def.text: Payoffs $\begin{pmatrix}(a,a)&(b,c)\\(c,b)&(d,d)\end{pmatrix}$ with $a>c\ge d>b$; pure equilibria are $(S,S)$ and $(H,H)$.
With $(a,b,c,d)=(5,0,4,3)$, stag hunting yields $5$ if both cooperate; hare hunting gives $3$ safely.

int.text: Everyone gains more by hunting the stag together, but hunting hare alone is safer.
Tension between payoff-dominance (stag) and risk-dominance (hare).

ex.text: A team project where collective effort yields $5$ each, individual effort $3$, and being the lone cooperator $0$.
International environmental agreements where only joint effort protects a common resource.

form.text: Harsanyi-Selten: $(S,S)$ is payoff-dominant, $(H,H)$ is risk-dominant. Experimentally, risk-dominance often prevails.
Infinitely repeated play can select $(S,S)$ under sufficient patience.

app.text: The European Union's common currency is a Stag Hunt: all member states benefit from a stable euro (hunting the stag), but during crises, individual countries are tempted to pursue independent monetary policies (hunting the hare), risking currency union collapse.
Rousseau's original parable of cooperation.

chicken.name: Chicken Game
chicken.summary: An anti-coordination game where both players prefer to yield if the other stays the course.

def.text: Payoffs $\begin{pmatrix}(0,0)&(-1,1)\\(1,-1)&(-10,-10)\end{pmatrix}$: mutual swerve $(0,0)$, one yields $(\pm 1)$, mutual crash $(-10,-10)$.
Two pure Nash equilibria (one yields, the other does not) and one mixed.

int.text: Commitment or bluster can force the rival to yield.
Miscoordination is catastrophic — both "holding" leads to disaster.

ex.text: Cuban Missile Crisis is a textbook application: two nuclear powers on a collision course.
Legislative budget standoffs exhibit chicken-style brinkmanship.

form.text: Schelling (1960): visible commitment — such as throwing away your steering wheel — is a rational move that shifts the equilibrium in your favor.
Correlated equilibrium strictly improves average welfare over all Nash equilibria.

app.text: The 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis is the definitive Chicken game: the US and USSR each preferred the other to back down, but mutual escalation meant nuclear war. Kennedy's naval blockade was a calibrated move short of full, irreversible commitment.

In labor disputes, both union and management may threaten a strike or lockout — each hoping the other will concede first. The risk is that both hold firm and the resulting work stoppage hurts everyone, just like a crash in Chicken.

matching_pennies.name: Matching Pennies
matching_pennies.summary: A zero-sum $2\\times 2$ game with no pure equilibrium and a unique uniform mixed equilibrium.

def.text: Payoff matrix $\begin{pmatrix}(1,-1)&(-1,1)\\(-1,1)&(1,-1)\end{pmatrix}$ where player 1 wins if the pennies match.
Unique Nash equilibrium: both mix $(1/2,1/2)$ with value $0$.

int.text: One player wins when the pennies match; the other when they mismatch.
Any deterministic strategy is exploitable — so both must randomize.

ex.text: Soccer penalty kicks between kicker and goalie approximate a matching-pennies structure; empirical studies find near-equilibrium randomization (Chiappori, Levitt, Groseclose).
Any $2\times 2$ game with cyclic best responses is strategically equivalent.

form.text: Canonical example of a game with no pure Nash equilibrium; demonstrates the necessity of mixed strategies.
Optimal randomization is derived via the minimax theorem.

app.text: Penalty kicks, serve direction in tennis, randomized audits, and cybersecurity patrolling.
Base case for teaching mixed-strategy equilibrium.

ultimatum.name: Ultimatum Game
ultimatum.summary: A two-stage bargaining game highlighting the gap between rational prediction and observed fairness.

def.text: Proposer offers a split $(x,1-x)$ of a pie; responder accepts (payoffs $(x,1-x)$) or rejects (payoffs $(0,0)$).
Subgame-perfect equilibrium: proposer offers $x\to 1$, responder accepts any $x<1$.

int.text: Self-interested rationality predicts a near-zero offer accepted; experiments show offers of $40$-$50$% and rejection of small offers.
Fairness, reciprocity, and spite enter the empirical picture.

ex.text: In the classical G\"uth-Schmittberger-Schwarze (1982) experiment, modal offers are $50/50$ and offers below $20$% are routinely rejected.
Dictator-game variants remove responder power to isolate pure altruism.

form.text: SPE predictions fail empirically — led to behavioral economics models such as inequity aversion (Fehr-Schmidt) and reciprocity (Rabin).
Evolutionary models with reputational tracking recover near-fair offers.

app.text: Behavioral economics, experimental game theory, and cross-cultural anthropology (Henrich et al.).
Foundational data point for bounded rationality research.

centipede.name: Centipede Game
centipede.summary: A multi-stage game where backward induction predicts immediate termination despite cooperative gains.

def.text: Players alternate; at each node a player can Take (ending the game) or Pass. Taking earlier yields a larger share; the pot grows if both Pass.
Backward induction: the last mover Takes, so the previous mover Takes, unraveling back to the first node.

int.text: Rational backward induction predicts immediate Take; experiments show extensive cooperation until near the end.
Challenges the plausibility of common knowledge of rationality in long chains.

ex.text: McKelvey-Palfrey (1992) found roughly half of subjects continued past the midpoint, contradicting the SPE prediction.
Rosenthal's original centipede has rapidly escalating payoffs making the paradox sharp.

form.text: SPE is the unique backward-induction outcome; level-$k$ and quantal-response models rationalize observed behavior.
Epistemic analyses show that $n$-fold common knowledge of rationality fails at long horizons.

app.text: Teaches the empirical limits of backward induction and motivates bounded-rationality refinements.
Widely used in experimental economics labs.

public_goods.name: Public-Goods Game
public_goods.summary: A game in which individuals contribute to a shared good; the efficient outcome is not individually rational.

def.text: Each of $n$ players contributes $c_i\in[0,w]$; public-good value is $\alpha\sum_j c_j/n$ for $\alpha\in(1,n)$; payoff $u_i=w-c_i+\alpha\sum_j c_j/n$.
Nash equilibrium has $c_i=0$; the efficient outcome has $c_i=w$ for all.

int.text: Free-riding is individually rational but collectively suboptimal.
A multiplayer generalization of the Prisoner's Dilemma.

ex.text: Voluntary-contribution experiments (Isaac-Walker) typically show $40$-$60$% contribution decaying toward zero without punishment.
Punishment opportunities sustain cooperation (Fehr-G\"achter).

form.text: Socially optimal contribution: $\sum c_i^*=nw$; Nash: $c_i=0$. The efficiency gap scales with $\alpha$ and $n$.
Linter-Samuelson condition characterizes optimal public-good provision: sum of MRS equals MRT.

app.text: Climate change mitigation is the canonical public-goods problem: each country benefits from global emission reductions but has incentives to free-ride on others' efforts. International agreements like the Paris Accord attempt to coordinate contributions.

Open-source software development exemplifies voluntary public-goods provision: developers contribute code that everyone can use, facing a temptation to free-ride while hoping others will maintain the project.

tragedy_commons.name: Tragedy of the Commons
tragedy_commons.summary: Overuse of a shared resource when individual users ignore the externality they impose on others.

def.text: Each of $n$ users chooses effort $e_i$; resource yield is $f(E)=\sum_i e_i g(E)$ for aggregate $E=\sum_j e_j$ with diminishing productivity.
Nash equilibrium yields $E^{NE}>E^*$; the social optimum demands $f'(E^*)=\text{marginal cost}$ accounting for externalities.

int.text: Each user considers only their own gain, ignoring the cost imposed on others through depletion.
Privately rational behavior aggregates into collective ruin.

ex.text: Overfishing in international waters and groundwater depletion are classic examples.
Traffic congestion is a commons-like equilibrium with excessive driving.

form.text: Equivalent to a public-bad game; Pigouvian taxation internalizes the externality to implement the social optimum.
Ostrom (1990) documented real-world governance institutions that sustain cooperation without privatization.

app.text: Global fisheries exemplify the tragedy of the commons: each fishing nation maximizes its own catch, but the aggregate harvest depletes fish stocks below sustainable levels. The UN Law of the Sea and catch-share systems attempt to internalize this externality.

Groundwater extraction by farmers illustrates the commons dilemma: each farmer drills deeper to secure water, but collective overpumping lowers the water table for all. California's Sustainable Groundwater Management Act mandates coordinated usage plans.

signaling.name: Signaling Games
signaling.summary: Dynamic incomplete-information games where an informed sender chooses a signal that a receiver observes.

def.text: Sender of type $\theta\in\Theta$ chooses signal $m\in M$; receiver observes $m$ and chooses action $a\in A$. Payoffs $u_S(m,a,\theta)$, $u_R(m,a,\theta)$; prior $p(\theta)$.
Solution concepts: separating, pooling, and semi-separating perfect Bayesian equilibria.

int.text: The informed party conveys information through costly action choices.
Equilibria are sustained by receiver beliefs that make deviation unprofitable for the sender.

ex.text: Spence (1973): education signals worker ability; separating equilibria have high-ability workers study more than low-ability workers would.
Costly signaling in biology (peacock tails) — the handicap principle.

form.text: Intuitive criterion (Cho-Kreps) refines PBE by restricting off-path beliefs.
Single-crossing / Spence-Mirrlees condition guarantees a unique separating equilibrium.

app.text: Job-market signaling, advertising, political posturing, and warranty design.
Central in information economics and biology.

screening.name: Screening Games
screening.summary: Games where an uninformed principal offers a menu of contracts to sort informed agents.

def.text: Principal offers menu $\{(q_\theta,t_\theta)\}_{\theta\in\Theta}$ satisfying IC ($\theta$ prefers $(q_\theta,t_\theta)$ to any other item) and IR.
In Rothschild-Stiglitz insurance screening, high-risk types take full insurance and low-risk types accept partial coverage.

int.text: The principal designs offers so that each type self-selects into a contract that reveals its type.
Incentive-compatibility and participation constraints shape the menu.

ex.text: Airline fare classes (fully refundable vs. non-refundable) screen business vs. leisure travelers.
Quantity discounts screen heavy vs. light users.

form.text: Taxation principle: any incentive-compatible allocation can be implemented through a price schedule, reducing screening to nonlinear pricing design.
Mussa-Rosen and Maskin-Riley derive optimal screening menus under single-crossing.

app.text: Insurance, second-degree price discrimination, optimal income taxation (Mirrlees), and credit rationing.
Dual of signaling — the uninformed party moves first.

principal_agent.name: Principal-Agent Problem
principal_agent.summary: A contracting problem where an agent\'s action is unobservable to the principal.

def.text: Principal chooses contract $w(y)$ based on observable output $y$; agent privately chooses effort $e$ affecting the distribution $F(y\mid e)$ at cost $c(e)$.
The problem is $\max_{w,e}\mathbb E[y-w(y)]$ s.t.\ IC ($e\in\arg\max_{e'}\mathbb E u(w)-c(e')$) and IR ($\mathbb E u(w)-c(e)\ge \bar u$).

int.text: Effort must be induced through output-contingent compensation that balances risk-sharing and incentives.
Information asymmetry requires trading off insurance for motivation.

ex.text: CEO compensation via stock options aligns manager and shareholder incentives but imposes risk.
Sharecropping (Stiglitz) splits output to induce effort when labor is unobservable.

form.text: Mirrlees-Holmstr\"om first-order approach: $1/u'(w(y))=\lambda+\mu\,\partial\ln f(y\mid e)/\partial e$ (informativeness principle).
Holmstr\"om (1979): any informative signal should be incorporated into the optimal contract.

app.text: Executive pay, procurement contracts, insurance, and regulation.
Cornerstone of contract theory.

folk.name: Folk Theorem
folk.summary: Any individually rational feasible payoff can be sustained in an infinitely repeated game with patient enough players.

def.text: Let $V$ be the convex hull of stage-game payoffs and $\underline v_i=\min_{\sigma_{-i}}\max_{\sigma_i}u_i(\sigma_i,\sigma_{-i})$ (min-max). Any $v\in V$ with $v_i>\underline v_i$ is a subgame-perfect equilibrium payoff for $\delta$ close enough to $1$.
Perfect Folk Theorem (Fudenberg-Maskin) handles the full-dimension and imperfect-monitoring cases.

int.text: Patience lets players threaten long-run punishments that sustain cooperation today.
Almost anything is an equilibrium — this is both a strength (explains cooperation) and weakness (weak predictions) of repeated games.

ex.text: In repeated Prisoner's Dilemma, grim-trigger sustains $(R,R)$ for $\delta\ge(T-R)/(T-P)$.
Aumann-Shapley (1994) provided the first characterization with observable mixed actions.

form.text: Classical proof constructs strategies with reward/punishment phases; requires individually rational payoffs and sufficient discount factors.
Versions exist for imperfect public monitoring (Abreu-Pearce-Stacchetti) and private monitoring (Kandori).

app.text: Rationalizes cartel stability, relational contracts, and community enforcement.
Explains why reputation and long relationships matter.

tft.name: Tit-for-Tat
tft.summary: A simple reciprocal strategy — cooperate first, then mimic the opponent\'s previous action.

def.text: Strategy in repeated Prisoner's Dilemma: $a_1=C$, $a_t=a_{t-1}^{(\text{opp})}$ for $t>1$.
Niceness (never defects first), retaliation, forgiveness (single punishment), and clarity are its defining traits.

int.text: Simple, provocable, forgiving, and clear — the four virtues that made it win Axelrod's tournaments.
Encourages cooperation without being exploitable by defectors.

ex.text: Axelrod's 1980 tournaments: Tit-for-Tat outperformed more complex entries in a round-robin of the repeated PD.
Generous and contrite variants handle noisy environments better.

form.text: Supports cooperation in the infinitely repeated PD as a subgame-perfect equilibrium when $\delta\ge(T-R)/(T-P)$.
In noisy environments TFT falls into retaliation spirals; Generous TFT (Nowak-Sigmund) repairs this.

app.text: Emerges in biology, diplomacy, and online reputation.
Remains a canonical benchmark in multi-agent learning.

backward_induction.name: Backward Induction
backward_induction.summary: Algorithm for solving finite perfect-information games by reasoning from terminal nodes back to the root.

def.text: Starting at terminal subgames, choose optimal actions for the moving player; propagate resulting payoffs upward to parent nodes and repeat.
The resulting strategy profile is the unique subgame-perfect equilibrium in generic finite perfect-information games.

int.text: Think about where the game ends first, then work backward to decide the current move.
It operationalizes sequential rationality.

ex.text: Chess (in principle, via Zermelo's theorem) is solved by backward induction; computationally it remains infeasible.
Stackelberg competition is solved by backward induction from the follower's best response.

form.text: Zermelo (1913): every finite two-player perfect-information game with no draws has a determined outcome — either a player wins or both can force a draw.
Equivalent to dynamic programming on the game tree.

app.text: Sequential bargaining, finite-horizon stopping problems, AI game-playing (minimax + alpha-beta pruning).
Bridge between game theory and dynamic programming.

ied.name: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies
ied.summary: Iteratively removing strategies that are strictly dominated.

def.text: Let $S_i^0=S_i$ and $S_i^{k+1}=\{s_i\in S_i^k:\nexists s_i'\in S_i^k\text{ s.t.\ } s_i'\text{ strictly dominates } s_i\text{ on }S_{-i}^k\}$; iterate.
A game is dominance-solvable if this process leaves a unique strategy profile.

int.text: Common knowledge of rationality lets us remove strictly dominated options round after round.
Predicts behavior when players reason about what rational opponents would never do.

ex.text: The Keynesian beauty contest / "p-guessing" game is dominance-solvable with unique outcome $0$.
Iterative dominance solves the traveler's dilemma at the minimum allowable value.

form.text: Iterated strict dominance preserves all Nash equilibria and yields a unique outcome independent of the order of elimination.
Iterated weak dominance depends on the order and can discard Nash equilibria.

app.text: Solution tool in auction theory and contract design; conceptual underpinning of rationalizability.
Foundation for level-$k$ models of strategic thinking.

rationalizability.name: Rationalizability
rationalizability.summary: The set of strategies surviving iterated elimination of strategies that are never a best response.

def.text: A strategy $s_i$ is rationalizable if it survives iterated deletion of never-best-responses, i.e., it is a best response to some belief over the surviving opponent strategies.
Formally the largest fixed point of the best-response operator.

int.text: A strategy is rationalizable if you can construct a chain of consistent beliefs that makes it optimal.
Weaker than Nash equilibrium — imposes common knowledge of rationality but not of beliefs.

ex.text: In a $2\times 2$ game with a strictly dominated action, only the dominant strategy is rationalizable.
In zero-sum games, rationalizable strategies coincide with minimax strategies.

form.text: Bernheim and Pearce (1984): rationalizability is the common-knowledge-of-rationality solution concept.
In two-player games, rationalizability equals iterated strict dominance.

app.text: Useful for analyzing games where beliefs about equilibrium play are hard to justify.
Foundation of epistemic game theory.

common_knowledge.name: Common Knowledge
common_knowledge.summary: An event is common knowledge if everyone knows it, everyone knows that everyone knows it, and so on ad infinitum.

def.text: Formally $E$ is common knowledge if $E\subseteq\bigcap_{n\ge 1}K^n E$, where $K E=\bigcap_i K_i E$ and $K_i$ denotes player $i$'s knowledge operator.
In a Kripke model, $E$ is common knowledge at state $\omega$ iff $E$ holds at every state reachable by the reflexive transitive closure of the agents' accessibility relations.

int.text: It is the infinite tower of mutual knowledge about a fact — not just you know, but you know I know, and I know you know I know, \u2026
Small failures of this tower can dramatically change strategic outcomes.

ex.text: Aumann's "Agreeing to Disagree": common priors plus common knowledge of posteriors implies equal posteriors.
The Muddy Children / Blue-Eyed Islanders puzzle hinges on common knowledge of the puzzle setup.

form.text: Rubinstein's e-mail game shows that approximate common knowledge is strictly weaker than full common knowledge.
Monderer-Samet characterize common knowledge via self-evident events.

app.text: Foundational for epistemic game theory, speculative trade, and cryptographic coordination.
Critical in the analysis of market microstructure and information cascades.

potential.name: Potential Games
potential.summary: Games where all unilateral payoff changes are captured by a single real-valued function.

def.text: $G$ is an exact potential game if there exists $\Phi:S\to\mathbb{R}$ with $u_i(s_i',s_{-i})-u_i(s_i,s_{-i})=\Phi(s_i',s_{-i})-\Phi(s_i,s_{-i})$ for all $i,s_i,s_i',s_{-i}$.
Ordinal, weighted, and generalized-ordinal potentials relax the equality requirement.

int.text: Players jointly hill-climb a common function — best-response dynamics always converge.
This gives potential games strong computational and learnability properties.

ex.text: Congestion games are exact potential games with $\Phi(s)=\sum_e\sum_{k=1}^{f_e(s)}c_e(k)$ (Rosenthal 1973).
Coordination games on graphs are (weighted) potential games.

form.text: Monderer-Shapley (1996): every finite potential game has a pure Nash equilibrium — the maximizer of $\Phi$.
Better-response and fictitious-play dynamics converge to Nash in potential games.

app.text: Distributed sensor networks use potential-game formulations to coordinate channel selection: each sensor's local decision to switch channels corresponds to climbing the potential function, guaranteeing convergence to interference-minimizing configurations.
Bridge to convex optimization via $\Phi$.

congestion.name: Congestion Games
congestion.summary: Games where each player\'s payoff depends on the load each facility carries.

def.text: Players choose subsets of facilities $E$; payoff $u_i(s)=-\sum_{e\in s_i}c_e(f_e(s))$ where $f_e(s)$ is the number of players using $e$.
Nonatomic (continuous flow) and atomic (discrete) variants yield Wardrop and Nash equilibria respectively.

int.text: Every facility's cost depends only on its total usage, not on who uses it.
Always a potential game, so pure equilibria always exist.

ex.text: Selfish routing on Pigou's network shows the Price of Anarchy $=4/3$ for linear latencies (Roughgarden-Tardos).
Job scheduling on identical machines is a congestion game.

form.text: Rosenthal (1973): every congestion game has the exact potential $\Phi(s)=\sum_e\sum_{k=1}^{f_e(s)}c_e(k)$, hence a pure Nash equilibrium.
Beckmann-McGuire-Winsten variational formulation gives Wardrop equilibrium as a convex program.

app.text: Internet routing (BGP), transportation planning, load balancing in data centers, and cloud resource allocation.
Core in algorithmic game theory.

poa.name: Price of Anarchy
poa.summary: The worst-case ratio between the social cost of a Nash equilibrium and the social optimum.

def.text: $\mathrm{PoA}(G)=\dfrac{\max_{s\in NE(G)} \mathrm{cost}(s)}{\min_{s}\mathrm{cost}(s)}$ for cost-minimization games.
Price of Stability uses $\min_{s\in NE(G)}$ instead.

int.text: Quantifies how much efficiency is lost due to selfish (non-cooperative) behavior.
Bounded PoA justifies decentralized design choices.

ex.text: For nonatomic selfish routing with linear latencies, PoA $=4/3$ (Pigou network is tight).
For atomic congestion games with linear costs, PoA $=5/2$ (Awerbuch-Azar-Epstein; Christodoulou-Koutsoupias).

form.text: Roughgarden's smoothness framework yields robust PoA bounds across many solution concepts (mixed, correlated, coarse-correlated).
Tight bounds often stem from Pigou-like example graphs.

app.text: Internet routing protocols like BGP let autonomous systems choose paths selfishly. The Price of Anarchy quantifies how much slower traffic becomes compared to an optimally coordinated routing scheme, guiding network design and traffic engineering.

In electricity markets, generators bid independently to maximize profit. The Price of Anarchy measures the efficiency loss from decentralized bidding relative to a centrally planned dispatch, informing market regulation and capacity planning.

zermelo.name: Zermelo\'s Theorem
zermelo.summary: In finite two-player zero-sum games of perfect information without chance and draws allowed, either one player has a winning strategy or both can force a draw.

def.text: For finite two-player zero-sum games of perfect information with only two outcomes (win/lose), exactly one player has a winning strategy.
With draws allowed, either one side wins or both can secure at least a draw.

int.text: Determinacy of perfect-information games — someone is guaranteed a good outcome.
Historically the founding result of game theory (Zermelo 1913).

ex.text: Chess: either White wins, Black wins, or both can force a draw — the game is determined, though we do not know which case.
Nim and other impartial games are fully solved (Sprague-Grundy theorem).

form.text: Proved by backward induction on the finite game tree.
Generalized to infinite games by Martin's Borel Determinacy Theorem.

app.text: Conceptual foundation for game-playing AI and combinatorial game theory.
Basis for alpha-beta pruning and minimax search.

harsanyi.name: Harsanyi Transformation
harsanyi.summary: Transforms games of incomplete information into games of imperfect information via a type space.

def.text: An incomplete-information game is reformulated as a Bayesian game $\langle N,\{\Theta_i\},p,\{A_i\},\{u_i\}\rangle$ with Nature drawing types $\theta\sim p$ and revealing $\theta_i$ to player $i$.
Under the common-prior assumption, all players share $p$; heterogeneous priors yield generalized Bayesian games.

int.text: Turns "I don't know what you know" into "I know the distribution of what you might know".
Enables equilibrium analysis of asymmetric-information games.

ex.text: Auctions with privately known valuations are modeled as Bayesian games via the Harsanyi transformation.
Signaling games and reputation models routinely apply it.

form.text: Harsanyi (1967-68) Econometrica trilogy; the common-prior assumption is often called the Harsanyi doctrine.
Mertens-Zamir constructed the universal type space ensuring a canonical model exists.

app.text: Auction theory, insurance, finance with asymmetric information, and mechanism design.
Essential conceptual tool in modern IO and information economics.

pareto.name: Pareto Efficiency
pareto.summary: An allocation is Pareto efficient if no player can be made strictly better off without making someone else worse off.

def.text: Allocation $a$ Pareto dominates $b$ if $u_i(a)\ge u_i(b)$ for all $i$ and strict for some $i$. An allocation is Pareto efficient if no alternative Pareto-dominates it.
The Pareto frontier is the set of all Pareto-efficient allocations.

int.text: It is the minimal standard for collective rationality: if everyone prefers $x$ to $y$, then $y$ should never be chosen.
Pareto efficiency is necessary but not sufficient for social optimality — it says nothing about distribution.

ex.text: In the Prisoner's Dilemma, $(C,C)$ Pareto-dominates $(D,D)$, yet $(D,D)$ is the unique Nash equilibrium.
Any competitive equilibrium in a market is Pareto efficient (First Welfare Theorem).

form.text: Mapping from strategy profiles to the payoff space $\mathbb{R}^n$, the Pareto frontier consists of maximal points under the componentwise partial order.
Scalarization: maximizing $\sum_i \lambda_i u_i$ for positive weights $\lambda$ traces the frontier for convex payoff sets.

app.text: Used as a welfare benchmark in every branch of economics — trade, regulation, taxation, and mechanism design.
Pareto improvements are the gold standard for policy evaluation.

sequential_eq.name: Sequential Equilibrium
sequential_eq.summary: A refinement of PBE requiring beliefs to be limits of fully mixed strategy perturbations.

def.text: An assessment $(\sigma,\mu)$ is a sequential equilibrium if (i) $\sigma$ is sequentially rational given $\mu$, and (ii) $\mu$ is consistent: there exist fully mixed $\sigma^n\to\sigma$ with $\mu^n\to\mu$ via Bayes' rule.
Consistency links off-path beliefs to the limit of nearby strategies, ruling out arbitrary belief assignments.

int.text: Sequential equilibrium demands that beliefs at every information set be the limit of beliefs from small trembles.
It is strictly stronger than PBE but weaker than trembling-hand perfection in the extensive form.

ex.text: Kreps-Wilson (1982) introduced sequential equilibrium to analyze the chain-store paradox and reputation in finitely repeated games.
In signaling games, sequential equilibrium plus the intuitive criterion selects reasonable separating equilibria.

form.text: Consistency requires a single sequence of completely mixed behavior strategies converging to $\sigma$; $\mu$ is the limit of the induced beliefs.
Kreps-Wilson existence: every finite extensive-form game has a sequential equilibrium.

app.text: Reputation models, signaling games, and dynamic games where off-path beliefs matter.
Standard refinement in applied economic theory.

proper_eq.name: Proper Equilibrium
proper_eq.summary: A refinement of trembling-hand perfection where more costly mistakes are infinitely less likely.

def.text: $\sigma^*$ is proper if there exists a sequence $\sigma^n\to\sigma^*$ of fully mixed strategies with $\sigma^n_i(s_i)\le \varepsilon^n \sigma^n_i(s_i')$ whenever $s_i$ is strictly worse than $s_i'$ against $\sigma^n_{-i}$, and $\varepsilon^n\to 0$.
Proper equilibria are always trembling-hand perfect and are invariant under elimination of strictly dominated strategies.

int.text: Players are infinitely more careful about costly errors: a mistake that hurts more is made with vanishingly smaller probability.
Myerson (1978) introduced the concept to address the limitations of trembling-hand perfection.

ex.text: In any normal-form game, proper equilibrium refines trembling-hand perfect by ruling out equilibria that rely on certain domination relationships.
A proper equilibrium always exists in every finite normal-form game (Myerson).

form.text: Every proper equilibrium is trembling-hand perfect, and every trembling-hand perfect equilibrium is proper in generic games.
Van Damme (1984): proper equilibria are characterized by a lexicographic hierarchy of caution.

app.text: Refined equilibrium selection in signaling and bargaining games.
Used in mechanism design to eliminate fragile Nash equilibria.

quantal_response.name: Quantal Response Equilibrium
quantal_response.summary: An equilibrium where players best-respond noisily with choice probabilities increasing in expected payoff.

def.text: In a QRE with logit errors, player $i$ chooses $s_i$ with probability $\exp(\lambda u_i(s_i,\sigma_{-i})) / \sum_{s_i'}\exp(\lambda u_i(s_i',\sigma_{-i}))$, where $\lambda\ge 0$ is the precision parameter.
The fixed-point condition: $\sigma_i^*(s_i) = \Pr(\text{choose } s_i \mid u_i(\cdot,\sigma_{-i}^*))$ under a stochastic choice model.

int.text: Players are more likely to choose better strategies but make errors with probability decreasing in payoff differences.
As $\lambda\to\infty$, QRE converges to a Nash equilibrium; as $\lambda\to 0$, it converges to uniform randomization.

ex.text: McKelvey-Palfrey (1995) showed QRE fits laboratory data from the centipede game and signaling games far better than Nash.
QRE explains why subjects in zero-sum games mix with slight deviations from the exact Nash prediction.

form.text: The logit QRE is the most common specification; nested families include power-function and payoff-monotone QRE.
Structural estimation of $\lambda$ from experimental data measures the degree of strategic sophistication.

app.text: Behavioral game theory, experimental economics, and estimation of bounded rationality.
Used to predict behavior in auctions, contests, and coordination games.

level_k.name: Level-k Reasoning
level_k.summary: A bounded-rationality model where players best-respond to lower-level opponents in a hierarchy of strategic depth.

def.text: Level-0 players choose uniformly or heuristically; level-$k$ players best-respond to level-$(k-1)$ opponents. A level-$\infty$ player best-responds to level-$(\infty-1)$ and converges to Nash.
The cognitive hierarchy model (Camerer-Ho-Chong) generalizes this with a Poisson distribution of levels.

int.text: Real people do not do infinite strategic reasoning — they guess, then guess what others guess, stopping at 1-3 steps.
Level-1: "I want the best outcome given that everyone else plays randomly." Level-2: "They think level-1, so I should anticipate that."

ex.text: In the "p-beauty contest" game (Nagel 1995), subjects' choices cluster around level-1 and level-2 reasoning, with very few reaching the Nash equilibrium of $0$.
In the centipede game, level-1 explains early defection; higher levels explain deeper cooperation.

form.text: The distribution of levels is often fitted as Poisson with mean $\tau\approx 1.5$ in lab experiments.
Level-$k$ differs from iterated dominance in that it is a behavioral model, not a normative refinement.

app.text: Experimental economics, auction bidding behavior, and financial market speculation.
Foundation for behavioral game theory alongside QRE.

global_games.name: Global Games
global_games.summary: Games of incomplete information where players observe noisy signals of an underlying state, often producing unique equilibrium.

def.text: Players receive private signals $x_i = \theta + \varepsilon_i$ where $\theta\in\mathbb{R}$ is the underlying state and $\varepsilon_i$ are i.i.d. noise with small support. Equilibrium thresholds $x^*$ solve indifference given posterior beliefs.
Carlsson-van Damme (1993): in $2\times 2$ games with strategic complementarities, the global game selects a unique equilibrium as noise vanishes.

int.text: Introducing tiny amounts of private information can break the multiplicity of coordination games.
The global-game selection (the "risk-dominant" action) emerges naturally from higher-order uncertainty.

ex.text: In a currency-attack model, traders observe noisy signals about fundamentals; the unique threshold equilibrium explains contagion and self-fulfilling crises (Morris-Shin 1998).
Bank runs, debt crises, and R&D investment have been studied as global games.

form.text: As signal noise $\sigma\to 0$, the equilibrium converges to a unique threshold generally selecting the risk-dominant action.
Laplacian belief: at the critical signal, players are uniform about the state — the "global-game" prior.

app.text: Macro-finance (currency crises, bank runs), political economy (revolutions), and industrial organization (entry).
Connects to the uniqueness of equilibrium in supermodular games.

supermodular.name: Supermodular Games
supermodular.summary: A game in which a player\'s incentive to raise their action increases with opponents\' actions (strategic complements).

def.text: A smooth game is supermodular if $\partial^2 u_i / \partial s_i \partial s_j \ge 0$ for all $i\ne j$. For general lattices, $u_i$ has increasing differences in $(s_i,s_{-i})$ and the strategy space is a complete lattice.
The set of Nash equilibria forms a nonempty complete lattice with a largest and smallest element (Topkis, 1979).

int.text: Actions are complements: if your rival does more, I want to do more too. This contrasts with Cournot (substitutes).
Strategic complements produce monotone best responses — the foundation for comparative statics without uniqueness.

ex.text: Bertrand competition with differentiated goods (prices are complements), bank-run games, and network adoption.
Research joint ventures and arms races exhibit strategic complements.

form.text: Topkis-Milgrom-Roberts monotonicity: if best responses are increasing, increasing the opponent's action raises your optimal action.
Milgrom-Roberts (1990): supermodular games have monotone comparative statics and converge under fictitious play.

app.text: Industrial organization, macroeconomics, and network effects.
The natural framework for comparative statics in oligopoly and coordination.

wardrop.name: Wardrop Equilibrium
wardrop.summary: A flow assignment in which every used path has equal cost and no unused path has lower cost.

def.text: A flow $f$ on a directed graph with latency functions $\ell_e(f_e)$ is a Wardrop equilibrium if for each O-D pair, all used paths $p$ have equal latency $L_p(f)=\sum_{e\in p}\ell_e(f_e)$ and no unused path has strictly lower latency.
Beckmann-McGuire-Winsten (1956): Wardrop equilibrium minimizes the potential $\sum_e\int_0^{f_e}\ell_e(t)\,dt$.

int.text: Drivers distribute themselves so that no individual can reduce their travel time by switching routes.
It is the Nash equilibrium of a nonatomic congestion game (continuum of infinitesimal agents).

ex.text: Pigou's two-link network: one fast road ($\ell(x)=1$) and one that congests ($\ell(x)=x$). Wardrop splits flow $1/2$-$1/2$; socially optimal flow is $1/4$-$3/4$ giving PoA $=4/3$.
Braess's paradox: adding a link can increase equilibrium travel time for all.

form.text: Variational inequality formulation: find $f^*$ such that $\sum_e\ell_e(f_e^*)(f_e-f_e^*)\ge 0$ for all feasible $f$.
Equivalent to convex optimization: $\min \sum_e\int_0^{f_e}\ell_e(t)\,dt$ subject to flow conservation.

app.text: Transportation planning, Internet routing (BGP/OSPF), and communication network design.
Foundational in algorithmic game theory and the Price of Anarchy literature.

price_of_stability.name: Price of Stability
price_of_stability.summary: The best-case ratio between the social cost of a Nash equilibrium and the social optimum.

def.text: $\mathrm{PoS}(G)=\dfrac{\min_{s\in NE(G)} \mathrm{cost}(s)}{\min_{s}\mathrm{cost}(s)}$ — the ratio of the best Nash equilibrium to the social optimum.
Always $\ge 1$ and $\le \mathrm{PoA}$. It measures whether there exists an acceptably efficient equilibrium.

int.text: While PoA looks at worst-case selfish outcomes, PoS checks if coordination failure is guaranteed or avoidable.
A low PoS means that even if agents play selfishly, the best equilibrium may still be near-efficient.
