ex.text: For network design (Shapley cost-sharing), PoS is $O(\log n)$ while PoA can be as high as $n$ (Anshel`evich et al. 2004).
In congestion games with linear costs, PoS $= 1$ (the best equilibrium is optimal).

form.text: Anshel`evich et al. (2008) gave the first general PoS bounds for network cost-sharing games.
The smoothness framework can bound PoA but not always PoS; potential methods are often used.

app.text: Network design, cost-sharing, and mechanism design where the designer can recommend an equilibrium.
Complements PoA in the efficiency analysis of games.

smoothed_poa.name: Smoothed Analysis of Price of Anarchy
smoothed_poa.summary: A framework showing that worst-case PoA bounds can be overly pessimistic — typical-case bounds are often dramatically better.

def.text: In smoothed analysis, inputs are perturbed by small random noise; the smoothed PoA is the expected (or high-probability) ratio under $\sigma$-perturbations. Roughgarden (2009) showed that strict Nash equilibria have near-optimal welfare under general conditions.
Smoothly-perturbed congestion games often have PoA close to 1, even when worst-case PoA is unbounded.

int.text: Worst-case PoA bounds rely on fragile constructions — tiny perturbations often eliminate the worst equilibria.
Smoothed analysis bridges the gap between worst-case theory and observed performance in practice.

ex.text: In atomic selfish routing, worst-case PoA can be $\Theta(n)$ for certain latencies, but smoothed PoA is $O(1)$.
In congestion games, adding small random noise to latencies makes nearly all Nash equilibria approximately efficient.

form.text: Roughgarden (2009) proved that for every $\epsilon>0$, the PoA of $\epsilon$-Nash equilibria in continuous nonatomic congestion games satisfies smoothed PoA $\le 1+\epsilon$.
The framework applies to any set of strategy profiles defined by equilibrium-like conditions that are robust to perturbation.

app.text: Theoretical justification for why practical routing, auctions, and markets perform better than worst-case bounds suggest.
Bridges algorithmic game theory and real-world traffic engineering.

no_regret.name: No-Regret Learning
no_regret.summary: Learning dynamics where a player\'s cumulative payoff is no worse than the best fixed action in hindsight, up to a vanishing average error.

def.text: Cumulative regret of player $i$ after $T$ rounds is $R_i(T)=\max_{s_i^*}\sum_{t=1}^T u_i(s_i^*,s_{-i}^t)-\sum_{t=1}^T u_i(s_i^t,s_{-i}^t)$. A no-regret algorithm guarantees $R_i(T)=o(T)$ (sublinear).
Hannan (1957): there exist strategies ensuring $\lim_{T\to\infty} R_i(T)/T = 0$ in any finite game.

int.text: No-regret means you eventually do as well as any fixed strategy — you learn to avoid systematic exploitation.
It does not require knowledge of the game or opponents — only observed payoffs.

ex.text: Multiplicative weights / Hedge algorithm achieves regret $O(\sqrt{T\log n})$ in any finite action set.
In a repeated matching-pennies game, no-regret play converges to the minimax value.

form.text: Hart-Mas-Colell (2000): if all players use no-regret algorithms, empirical play converges to the set of correlated equilibria.
"No internal regret" and "no swap regret" characterize convergence to correlated and coarse-correlated equilibria.

app.text: Online learning, multi-agent systems, algorithmic game theory, and AI training (regret-based policy improvement).
Core theoretical foundation for multi-agent reinforcement learning.

fictitious_play.name: Fictitious Play
fictitious_play.summary: A learning process in which players best-respond to the empirical distribution of past opponent play.

def.text: At each round $t$, player $i$ forms beliefs $\hat\sigma_{-i}^t$ as the empirical frequency of opponents' past actions and best-responds: $s_i^t\in\arg\max_{s_i} u_i(s_i,\hat\sigma_{-i}^t)$.
Convergence in beliefs need not imply convergence in actions: the path can cycle.

int.text: Players track what opponents have done historically and choose the action that would have been best against that history.
It captures a simple form of experiential learning without Bayesian updating.

ex.text: Fictitious play converges to Nash in zero-sum games (Robinson 1951), potential games (Monderer-Shapley), and $2\times 2$ games with strategic complements.
In Rock-Paper-Scissors, fictitious play cycles without converging.

form.text: Brown (1951): originally proposed as a method to compute the value of zero-sum games.
Fictitious play is a best-response dynamic; smooth fictitious play replaces argmax with stochastic choice (QRE-like).

app.text: Algorithmic game theory (computing equilibria), multi-agent learning, and economic modeling of boundedly rational learning.
Connects to reinforcement learning and regret minimization.

evol_stable_sets.name: Evolutionarily Stable Sets
evol_stable_sets.summary: A set of strategies such that every strategy in the set resists invasion by any outside mutant.

def.text: A set $A\subseteq \Delta(S)$ is evolutionarily stable if (i) for every $x,y\in A$, $u(x,x)=u(y,x)=u(y,y)$ (equal payoffs within the set), and (ii) for every $x\in A$, $y\notin A$, there exists $\bar\varepsilon>0$ with $u(x,\,\varepsilon y+(1-\varepsilon)x)>u(y,\,\varepsilon y+(1-\varepsilon)x)$ for all $\varepsilon\in(0,\bar\varepsilon)$.
Thomas (1985) introduced ESSets to handle games where a single ESS does not exist but a set is collectively stable.

int.text: When no single strategy is unbeatable, a pool of behavior may collectively resist invasion.
Think of a set of social norms or behavioral conventions that mutually reinforce each other.

ex.text: In Rock-Paper-Scissors, the entire interior of the simplex is an evolutionarily stable set under certain payoff variants.
In the War of Attrition, any symmetric distribution over waiting times can form part of an ESSet.

form.text: An ESSet is a closed singleton if and only if it contains an ESS.
Neutrally stable strategies (NSS) form a weaker version: $u(x,y)\ge u(y,y)$ for all $y$ near $x$.

app.text: Understanding polymorphism in biology, cultural norms, and convention formation.
Models situations where multiple behaviors co-exist stably.

cheap_talk.name: Cheap Talk
cheap_talk.summary: Costless, non-binding, and verifiable communication prior to play that can influence equilibrium selection.

def.text: In the Crawford-Sobel (1982) model, a privately informed sender sends a costless, non-binding message; the receiver then chooses an action. Because messages are costless, they are "cheap" — only the sender's preferences link messages to types.
Cheap talk can be informative only if sender and receiver preferences are sufficiently aligned (the "no-babbling" condition).

int.text: Words cost nothing, so why believe them? Cheap talk works only when lying would hurt the liar's credibility.
Partial alignment of interests makes some information transmission possible — the "babbling" equilibrium (no information) always exists.

ex.text: A manager making a forecast to investors: if both want the stock price to reflect true value, the forecast can be credible.
Political endorsements: if both voter and candidate want good policy, the signal conveys information.

form.text: In the CS interval model with one-dimensional types and quadratic preferences, there exist informative equilibria partitioned into $N$ intervals when preference divergence $b$ is not too large.
The number of partition elements in the most informative equilibrium decreases as preference divergence grows.

app.text: Political communication, corporate disclosures, advertising, and pre-trial negotiation.
Foundational model in information economics and political science.

berge_eq.name: Berge Equilibrium
berge_eq.summary: An equilibrium where each player maximizes the payoff of all others, assuming they do not deviate from their Berge strategies.

def.text: A strategy profile $s^*$ is a Berge equilibrium if for each player $i$, $u_i(s_i^*,s_{-i}^*)\ge u_i(s_i,s_{-i}^*)$ for all $s_i\in S_i$ (Nash condition), and additionally $u_i(s_i^*,s_{-i}^*)\ge u_i(s_i^*,s_{-i})$ for all $s_{-i}\in S_{-i}$ (Berge condition).
The second condition says player $i$'s payoff is maximized at $s^*$ given $i$ plays their part — the opponent's choices do not harm $i$ at the equilibrium.

int.text: In a Berge equilibrium, each player takes the action that is best for the others, creating a kind of altruistic balance.
It contrasts with Nash (self-interested) and provides a model of "I'll do what's good for you, trusting you'll do what's good for me."

ex.text: In a two-player coordination game with Pareto-ranked equilibria, the payoff-dominant equilibrium is often also a Berge equilibrium.
Team sports: each player positions themselves to benefit teammates, relying on teammates to do the same.

form.text: Every Nash equilibrium that is also Pareto efficient and satisfies $u_i(s_i^*,s_{-i}^*)=\max_{s_{-i}}u_i(s_i^*,s_{-i})$ for all $i$ is a Berge equilibrium.
Berge (1957) introduced the concept; Colman (2006) revived it as a model of team reasoning.

app.text: Team decision-making, cooperative behavior in social dilemmas, and evolutionary models of altruism.
Alternative to Nash in settings with group-oriented reasoning.

mean_field.name: Mean Field Game
mean_field.summary: A game with a continuum of players where each player\'s influence on aggregate is negligible, simplifying equilibrium to a PDE system.

def.text: In a mean-field game, the state of the game is the distribution $m_t$ of agents over states. Each agent solves a Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation $\partial_t u + H(x,\nabla u, m) = 0$, and the population evolves via the Fokker-Planck equation $\partial_t m - \mathrm{div}(m\,\partial_p H) = 0$. The system $(u,m)$ forms the MFG equilibrium.
Lasry-Lions (2006) and Huang-Caines-Malhamé (2006) independently developed the theory.

int.text: With millions of agents, no individual can affect aggregate — but all are affected by it. The equilibrium couples optimal control (forward by agents) and population dynamics (forward in time).
MFG reduces a massive interactive system to two coupled PDEs, making analysis tractable.

ex.text: Taxi fleets: each driver chooses a location; the distribution of all drivers determines earnings. The MFG equilibrium captures spatial equilibrium.
Emission models: each firm decides abatement; aggregate emissions determine climate damage.

form.text: A stationary MFG: $\lambda + H(x,\nabla u, m) = 0$ and $\mathrm{div}(m\,\partial_p H)=0$, solved together with boundary conditions.
Existence and uniqueness of classical solutions require monotonicity of the Hamiltonian in $m$ (Lasry-Lions).

app.text: Economics (labor markets, wealth distribution), finance (systemic risk), engineering (autonomous driving), and epidemiology.
Active frontier in applied mathematics and algorithmic game theory.

algo_mech_design.name: Algorithmic Mechanism Design
algo_mech_design.summary: The interface of mechanism design and computer science, concerned with computational tractability of incentive-compatible outcomes.

def.text: Algorithmic mechanism design (AMD) studies the design of mechanisms whose outcome functions are polynomial-time computable, particularly when the social objective (e.g., welfare-maximizing allocation) is computationally hard.
Nisan-Ronen (1999) introduced AMD, focusing on algorithmic tractability, approximation, and communication efficiency.

int.text: Classical mechanism design assumes the center can compute the optimal outcome. AMD asks: what if computing the outcome is NP-hard? Can we design computationally efficient, incentive-compatible approximations?
It brings together algorithmic analysis, game theory, and computational complexity.

ex.text: Combinatorial auctions: allocating many items to bidders with complex preferences is NP-hard. AMD designs polynomial-time, incentive-compatible auction algorithms (e.g., greedy bundle auctions).
VCG mechanisms are not always computationally tractable; AMD develops computationally efficient VCG-like mechanisms.

form.text: Key tools: approximation algorithms (polynomial-time with near-optimal guarantees), convex programming-based mechanisms, and communication complexity lower bounds.
AMD also studies "mechanism design without money" where payments are not allowed (e.g., matching, voting).

app.text: Internet ad auctions (GSP, VCG), cloud resource allocation, spectrum auctions, and kidney exchange.
Foundational for the theory of electronic marketplaces.

fair_division.name: Fair Division
fair_division.summary: The study of how to divide resources among agents so that each receives a fair share according to some criterion.

def.text: An allocation is proportional if each of $n$ agents receives at least $1/n$ of the total value by their own measure. It is envy-free if no agent prefers another's bundle to their own.
Fair division problems are categorized as divisible (cake-cutting) or indivisible (goods/items), and as homogeneous or heterogeneous.

int.text: Fair division asks: what does each person deserve, and how can algorithms guarantee it without knowing their private valuations?
The canonical cake-cutting problem asks for a protocol guaranteeing each player a piece they value at $\ge 1/n$ of the whole.

ex.text: The "I cut, you choose" protocol for two players guarantees envy-free division.
Dubins-Spanier (1961) moving-knife protocol achieves proportional division for $n$ players.

form.text: The Robertson-Webb query model formalizes cake-cutting algorithms that ask "evaluate" and "cut" queries.
Envy-free division for $n$ players with arbitrary valuations requires a number of queries that is $\Theta(n\log n)$ (Even-Paz) or more, and is not guaranteed to exist for indivisible goods.

app.text: Estate settlement, divorce proceedings, international treaty negotiations (e.g., maritime boundaries), and resource allocation in computing.
Increasingly applied in AI for multi-agent resource allocation.

voting_theory.name: Voting Theory
voting_theory.summary: The study of procedures for aggregating individual preferences into a collective decision.

def.text: A voting rule $F: \mathcal{P}^n \to A$ maps each profile of ranked or scored preferences over alternatives to a winner. Common rules: plurality, Borda count, instant-runoff (IRV), approval voting, Condorcet methods.
Key evaluation criteria: Pareto efficiency, Condorcet consistency, independence of irrelevant alternatives (IIA), strategy-proofness.

int.text: No voting rule is perfect — Arrow's theorem shows any reasonable rule must violate at least one basic desideratum.
Different rules can produce wildly different outcomes from the same preferences, so the choice of rule is itself a political decision.

ex.text: In the 2000 US presidential election, third-party candidate Ralph Nader split the vote, illustrating spoiler effects in plurality voting.
Borda count assigns points by rank position; it's used in Eurovision and some academic elections.

form.text: Arrow (1951): no social welfare function for $\ge 3$ alternatives simultaneously satisfies unrestricted domain, Pareto efficiency, IIA, and non-dictatorship.
The Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem: every non-dictatorial voting rule with $\ge 3$ alternatives is manipulable (not strategy-proof).

app.text: Political elections, committee decision-making, collaborative filtering, and multi-agent preference aggregation.
Foundational in social choice theory and political science.

social_choice.name: Social Choice Theory
social_choice.summary: The theoretical study of how individual preferences and judgments can be aggregated into collective decisions.

def.text: Social choice theory studies mappings from individual preferences, judgments, or welfare to collective outcomes: $f: \mathcal{P}^n \to X$, where $X$ is the set of social alternatives.
Core results: Arrow's impossibility theorem, Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem, Sen's liberal paradox, and the median voter theorem.

int.text: It reveals the logical limits of democracy and collective rationality — no aggregation method satisfies all desirable properties.
Social choice sits at the intersection of economics, political science, and philosophy.

ex.text: Condorcet's paradox: majority preferences can cycle ($A$ beats $B$, $B$ beats $C$, $C$ beats $A$) even with rational voters.
Amartya Sen's liberal paradox: commitment to minimal liberty and Pareto efficiency can conflict.

form.text: Arrow's theorem: for $|X|\ge 3$, any SWF satisfying Pareto and IIA must be a dictatorship.
The Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem: any non-dictatorial social choice function for $|X|\ge 3$ is manipulable.

app.text: Constitutional design, voting system analysis, welfare economics, and multi-agent systems.
Central to understanding the mathematical foundations of democracy.

banzhaf.name: Banzhaf Power Index
banzhaf.summary: A measure of a voter\'s ability to influence the outcome of a vote, calculated as the number of coalitions in which they are pivotal.

def.text: In a weighted voting game $[q; w_1,\ldots,w_n]$, a voter $i$ is critical (pivotal) for a coalition $S$ if adding $i$ to $S$ makes $S$ winning ($w(S)<q \le w(S)+w_i$). The Banzhaf index is $\beta_i = \eta_i / \sum_j \eta_j$, where $\eta_i$ is the number of coalitions in which $i$ is critical.
The absolute Banzhaf index is $\eta_i / 2^{n-1}$ (proportion of coalitions where $i$ is pivotal).

int.text: A voter's power is not their share of votes but their chance of being the deciding vote.
Banzhaf (1965) used this to show that the weighted voting system of Nassau County's Board of Supervisors gave some districts zero power despite having votes.

ex.text: In the EU Council with triple majority rules, Banzhaf indices show that large countries have disproportionately high power relative to population.
In a shareholder election with 49% vs 51%, the 51% bloc has essentially all the power despite having only 2% more shares.

form.text: Related to, but distinct from, the Shapley-Shubik index, which orders voters by random arrival rather than random coalitions.
Computing Banzhaf indices is #P-complete in general but tractable for small or structured games.

app.text: Constitutional analysis, corporate governance, shareholder voting, and international treaty ratification.
Used in legal challenges to voting systems on equal-protection grounds.

core_selection.name: Core Selection (Matching)
core_selection.summary: The selection of an allocation from the core of a matching market, typically via deferred-acceptance algorithms.

def.text: In a two-sided matching market (e.g., men-women, workers-firms), a matching $\mu$ is in the core if no blocking pair $(m,w)$ exists such that both prefer each other to their current partners under $\mu$.
Gale-Shapley (1962) deferred acceptance algorithm finds the firm-optimal (or worker-optimal) stable matching in polynomial time.

int.text: The core in matching corresponds to stability: no pair wants to leave their current match for each other.
Deferred acceptance simulates a market where one side proposes and the other provisionally holds the best offer, iteratively improving.

ex.text: The National Resident Matching Program (NRMP) uses a deferred-acceptance algorithm to match medical residents to hospitals. The resulting match is stable (core-selected).
School choice programs in New York and Boston use variants of the Gale-Shapley mechanism.

form.text: The set of stable matchings forms a distributive lattice with the man-optimal and woman-optimal matchings as extreme points.
The core is nonempty in any two-sided matching market with strict preferences (Gale-Shapley). In one-sided (roommate) problems, the core may be empty.

app.text: Labor market matching, school assignment, kidney exchange, and online dating platforms.
Winner of the 2012 Nobel Memorial Prize (Shapley and Roth).
